policy_statement: Adopt legislation to prohibit or restrict anti-concurrent causation clauses for sewer backup insurance where flooding is the cause.
mechanism_description: Adopt legislation to prohibit or restrict anti-concurrent causation clauses in sewer backup insurance so flooding-related sewer backup losses are more likely to be covered, improving financial resilience after floods.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → insurance coverage/claims certainty → ↓ financial exposure + ↑ recovery capacity
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Legal/contractual rules governing sewer-backup insurance (restricting anti-concurrent causation clauses) so more flood-caused sewer backup losses qualify for coverage.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased claim eligibility and payout reliability transfers/redistributes financial risk from households/businesses to insurers/reinsurers and improves post-event liquidity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower economic losses borne by affected parties, reduced disaster-induced financial stress/displacement, and faster recovery after flood events (i.e., improved resilience to climate-amplified flooding).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced financial vulnerability and faster recovery from flood impacts by increasing the likelihood and adequacy of insurance payouts for sewer-backup losses.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Flood hazard occurs → policy reform increases likelihood of insurance coverage for sewer backup → reduces exposed parties’ financial losses and improves recovery.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The main intended outcome is resilience to flood impacts (risk transfer and financial recovery), not emissions reduction (Mitigation), reduced resource/energy inputs (Resource Efficiency), or ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: While better insurance terms could indirectly influence rebuilding decisions or risk-taking, those are secondary/ambiguous and not the principal climate effect of the mechanism; the direct, consistent effect is financial resilience.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that restricts anti-concurrent causation clauses (or similar exclusions) for flood-related sewer backup is fundamentally about improving post-flood financial protection, so Adaptation remains primary across implementations.  
5) Secondary subtype note: More granular sub-classification (e.g., “risk transfer,” “recovery financing,” “financial resilience”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and taxonomy rules beyond this primary-category assignment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible moral-hazard/induced development effects (if expanded coverage encourages continued occupancy in flood-prone areas) and distributional impacts (premiums/affordability) could alter net resilience outcomes, but they do not change the mechanism’s primary classification as Adaptation.
key_indicators: “adopt legislation”; “prohibit or restrict”; “anti-concurrent causation clauses”; “sewer backup insurance coverage”; “where flooding is the cause”
co_benefits: Improved general consumer protection in insurance contracts (beyond climate-driven floods); reduced litigation/claims disputes over causation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be framed as a performance standard (minimum coverage/claims eligibility) in some implementations, but the text explicitly emphasizes prohibiting/restricting clause language via legislation, aligning best with a regulatory mandate.
